title: How does harf work?
text: R package harf extends adversarial random forests (ARFs) high-dimensional data. vignette serves user guide use package effectively. Two key functionalities provided: h_arf train estimate densities high-dimensional adversarial random forest ({h}-ARF), h_forge synthetic data generating process. Unconditional conditional data generating processes supported. package designed handle high-dimensional omics data, gene expression measurements, can applied various downstream analyses, including clustering (unsupervised) classification generate synthetic data (supervised). clustering, illustrate usage harf package using single-cell RNA-seq dataset, goal generate synthetic data preserves underlying structure original data. prediction, illustration made based gene expression data, goal synthesize gene expression data can used assess performance prediction models absence original datasets. situation typically arise research face sample size limitation enough data built evaluate prediction model.

title: How does harf work?
text: package single cell built-dataset single_cell used vignette originate Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) Genotype-Tissue Expression (GTEx) projects, two large-scale public resources providing extensive RNA-seq data. data extracted previously processed curated datasets generated using pipeline described Aguet et al. (2017). pipeline ensures log-transformed gene measurements, expressed least 6%6\% cells. keep computational burden manageable vignette, randomly selected 8080 genes 16521652 original dataset. included cells grouped human organes, including bladder, breast, cervix, colon, esophagus (gastroesophageal junction, mucosa, muscularis), kidney, liver, lung, prostate, salivary gland, stomach, thyroid, uterus. variable cell_type indicates tissue origin cell. following code requires packages installed. load required libraries. genetic epidemiology studies, molecular (omics) data typically accompanied clinical phenotypic variables provide essential contextual information. h-ARF framework assumes input data consist two components: () high-dimensional numeric omics features (ii) associated clinical phenotypic variables, may mixed (categorical numeric). following example, gene expression measurements used omics data, cell type serves labor information. first aim train generative model generate synthetic data preserves underlying structure original data, including correlation structure features cluster structure cells. set chunk size size 5 illustration, practice, users may need tune parameter achieve predefined level, given performance measure.
code: install.packages("data.table") install.packages("rsvd") install.packages("Rtsne") install.packages("cowplot") if (!require("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE))   install.packages("BiocManager") BiocManager::install("SingleCellExperiment") BiocManager::install("scater") install.packages("ggplot2") install.packages("corrplot") install.packages("doParallel") library(harf) library(data.table) library(rsvd) library(Rtsne) library(cowplot) library(SingleCellExperiment) library(ggplot2) library(corrplot) library(scater) # Register cores - Unix library(doParallel) registerDoParallel(cores = 2) # Set seed set.seed(12, "L'Ecuyer-CMRG") data("single_cell") chunk_size <- 5

title: How does harf work?
text: train {h}-ARF model using h_arf function. gene expression measurements provided omx_data argument, cell type information passed cli_lab_data argument. set maximal chunk size 5, specify maximal number features allowed isolated region. parameter crucial three main aspects, including () controlling convergence ARF isolated regions, (ii) learning joint pattern features, (iii) managing runtime.
code: my_omx_data <- single_cell[ , - which(colnames(single_cell)  == "cell_type")] my_cli_lab_data <- data.frame(cell_type = single_cell$cell_type) harf_model <- h_arf(  omx_data = my_omx_data,  cli_lab_data = my_cli_lab_data,  feature_ordering = colnames(single_cell),  parallel = FALSE,  chunk_size = chunk_size,  verbose = TRUE ) #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 78.67% #> Iteration: 1, Accuracy: 1, Accuracy: 48.29% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 86.4% #> Iteration: 1, Accuracy: 45.87% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 80.58% #> Iteration: 1, Accuracy: 44.95% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 85.89% #> Iteration: 1, Accuracy: 50.34% #> Iteration: 2, Accuracy: 45.2% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 88.68% #> Iteration: 1, Accuracy: 56.51% #> Iteration: 2, Accuracy: 46.5% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 84.89% 46.89% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 87.93% #> Iteration: 1, Accuracy: 51.73% #> Iteration: 2, Accuracy: 45.03% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 82.82% #> Iteration: 1, Accuracy: 49.15% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 85.08% #> Iteration: 1, Accuracy: 48.64% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 87.08% #> Iteration: 1, Accuracy: 48.28% str(harf_model,max.level = 1) #> List of 10 #>  $ meta_model       :List of 3 #> omx_features     : chr [1:80] "V1" "V2" "V3" "V4" ... #>  $ cli_lab_features : chr "cell_type" #>  $ omx_constant_data: NULL #>  $ feature_ordering : chr [1:81] "cell_type" "V1" "V2" "V3" ... #>  $ accuracy         : Named num [1:17] 0.44 0.484 0.468 0.473 0.463 ... #>   ..- attr(*, "names")= chr [1:17] "meta_model" "cluster_2" "cluster_3" "cluster_4" ... #>  - attr(*, "class")= chr "harf"

title: How does harf work?
text: plot accuracy {h}-ARF model across training regions meta region ensure adversarial game converged properly. accuracy lesser 0.50.5 indicates ARF model locally converged, .e. stopped able distinguish original synthetic data. contrast, accuracy larger 0.50.5 indicates ARF locally converge. recommend set chunk size parameter local ARF model converge.
code: acc_df <- data.frame(   Region = names(harf_model$accuracy),   Accuracy = harf_model$accuracy ) acc_plot <- ggplot2::ggplot(acc_df, ggplot2::aes(x = Region, y = Accuracy)) +   ggplot2::geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +   ggplot2::geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "steelblue") +   ggplot2::ylim(0, 1) +   ggplot2::labs(title = "h-ARF Convergence Accuracy",                 x = "Regions",                 y = "Accuracy") +   ggplot2::theme_minimal() +   ggplot2::theme(axis.text.x = ggplot2::element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)) acc_plot

title: How does harf work?
text: use h_forge function generate sznthsize single cell data using trained {h}-ARF model. , generate number synthetic samples original dataset, without evidence, .e. without prio information regarding single cell classes (evidence = NULL). see later, evidence argument can used generate synthetic data conditionally specific cell types. generated synthetic data stored synth_single_cell object.
code: set.seed(32) synth_single_cell <- h_forge(   harf_obj = harf_model,   n_synth = nrow(single_cell),    evidence = NULL,   parallel = FALSE,   verbose = FALSE   )

title: How does harf work?
text: visually compare correlation matrices original data synthetic data. enhance interpretability, rearrange features according region assignments obtained {h}-ARF model. correlation plots exhibit similar patterns across three datasets, indicating {h}-ARF model effectively preserve origin feature correlation structure.
code: # Re-arrange data by grouping gene by clusters cluster_feature <- copy(harf_model$cluster) setorder(cluster_feature, cluster) orig_clustered <- single_cell[ , c("cell_type", cluster_feature$feature)] synth_clustered <- as.data.frame(synth_single_cell)[ , c("cell_type", cluster_feature$feature)] plot_corr <- function(dt, title) {   corr_matrix <- cor(dt[ , 2:21], method = "spearman")   corrplot(corr_matrix,            method = "circle",            tl.col = "black",            tl.pos = "n",            title = title,            mar = c(0, 0, 1, 0)) }

what: Show original and synthetic data in 2D using t-SNE
title: How does harf work?
text: use t-SNE visualize cells two-dimensional space. Original cell clusters preserved synthetic data, indicating {h}-ARF model effectively captures underlying cluster structure original data.
code: tsne_it <- function (sc_data, perp = 30, title = "") {   # Create SingleCellExperiment object   sce <- SingleCellExperiment::SingleCellExperiment(     assays = list(counts = t(as.matrix(sc_data[ , - which(colnames(sc_data)  == "cell_type")])))   )   SingleCellExperiment::logcounts(sce) <- SingleCellExperiment::counts(sce) # Log-normalization   sce$cell_type <- sc_data$cell_type   pc_sce <- rpca(t(SingleCellExperiment::counts(sce)))   # tSNE with rotated pcs   ts_sce <- Rtsne::Rtsne(     pc_sce$x %*% pc_sce$rotation,     perplexity = perp,     verb = FALSE,     pca = FALSE,     check_duplicates = FALSE   )   SingleCellExperiment::reducedDim(sce, "tsne") = ts_sce$Y   sce_plot <- scater::plotReducedDim(sce, "tsne", colour_by = "cell_type") +    ggplot2::ggtitle(title) +   ggplot2::theme(legend.position = "bottom")   return(sce_plot) }   orig_plot <- tsne_it(single_cell,                      perp = 30,                      title = "Original") synth_plot <- tsne_it(as.data.frame(synth_single_cell),                       perp = 30,                       title = "Synthetic") legend <- cowplot::get_legend(   orig_plot + theme(legend.position = "bottom") ) orig_plot <- orig_plot + theme(legend.position = "none") synth_plot <- synth_plot + theme(legend.position = "none") all_plots <- cowplot::plot_grid(orig_plot,                                 synth_plot,                                 ncol = 2) par(mfrow = c(1,2)) plot_corr(orig_clustered, "Original") plot_corr(synth_clustered, "Synthetic") par(mfrow = c(1, 1)) print(all_plots)

title: How does harf work?
text: evidence parameter required conditional resampling. generate synthetic samples cell typ separately. generated samples combined form final synthetic dataset. example, synthesize lung, liver esophagus mucosa cell types. correlation structure cluster structure well preserved conditionally generated synthetic data.   , correlation structure cluster structure well preserved conditionally generated synthetic data.
code: sub_cell_type <- c("lung", "liver", "esophagus_muc") single_cell_list <- lapply(sub_cell_type, function (ct) {   ct_synth <- h_forge(         harf_obj = harf_model,         n_synth = sum(single_cell$cell_type == ct),         evidence = data.frame(cell_type = ct),         verbose = FALSE,         parallel = FALSE       )   return(ct_synth) }) cond_synth_single_cell <- do.call(rbind, single_cell_list) cond_synth_clustered <- as.data.frame(cond_synth_single_cell)[ , c("cell_type", cluster_feature$feature)] cond_synth_plot <- tsne_it(as.data.frame(cond_synth_single_cell),                            perp = 30,                            title = "Conditional resampling") sub_legend <- cowplot::get_legend(   cond_synth_plot + theme(legend.position = "none") ) cond_synth_plot <- cond_synth_plot + theme(legend.position = "none") sub_single_cell <- orig_clustered[orig_clustered$cell_type %in% sub_cell_type , ] sub_orig_plot <- tsne_it(sub_single_cell,                      perp = 30,                      title = "Original") legend <- cowplot::get_legend(   orig_plot + theme(legend.position = "bottom") ) sub_all_plots <- cowplot::plot_grid(sub_orig_plot,                                 cond_synth_plot,                                 ncol = 2) par(mfrow = c(1,2)) plot_corr(sub_single_cell, "Original") plot_corr(cond_synth_clustered, "Synthetic") par(mfrow = c(1, 1)) plot_grid(sub_all_plots, sub_legend, ncol = 1, rel_heights = c(1, 0.2)) par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

title: How does harf work?
text: goal generate synthetic gene expression data can used evaluate performance prediction model absence original testing datasets, situation researchers may enough samples eventual internal validation corss-validation. supervised downstream analysis example, consider Cancer Genome Atlas Kidney Chromophobe Collection (TCGA-KICH) gene expression predictors, artificial tumor stage binary response variable, since provided original tumor stages difficult predict. dataset contains 6666 samples top 140140 gene expression highest empirical variances. include age gender additional clinical variables. scale gene expressions age. create response variable, fix age first 1010 gene expression variables drive effect. set effect age 0.50.5, draw effects gene expressions uniformly interval [−1,1][-1, 1]. Subsequently, train {h}-ARF model using h_arf function training data, gene expression measurements provided omx_data argument, binary outcome variable addition variables including age gender (2727 males 3939 females) passed cli_lab_data argument. also specify target variable using parameter target. set maximal chunk size 1010, specify maximal number features allowed isolated region. training {h}-ARF model, use h_forge function generate synthetic training dataset. train prediction model, original synthetic training data evaluate performances common testing data. report area Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve (AUC) performance metric. expect prediction model trained synthetic data similar performance one trained original data, indicating {h}-ARF model effectively captures underlying structure original data can used generate realistic synthetic datasets prediction tasks. set required libraries. load kich dataset create training testing indices. use ranger package train random forest model original training data evaluate performance testing data.
code: library(data.table) library(pROC) # If not installed, use install.packages("pROC") to install it. library(caret) # If not installed, use install.packages("caret") to install it. library(ranger) # If not installed, use install.packages("ranger") to install it. seed <- 123 data("kich") set.seed(seed) train_idx <- caret::createDataPartition(   kich$tumor_stage,   p = 0.7,   list = FALSE ) train_idx <- train_idx[ , "Resample1"]

what: Prediction model trained on the original data
title: How does harf work?
code: set.seed(seed) rf_model <- ranger(tumor_stage ~ .,                    data = kich[train_idx, ],                    num.trees = 500,                    probability = FALSE) # Estimate AUC on the test set test_pred <- predict(rf_model, data = kich[-train_idx, ])$predictions test_labels <- kich$tumor_stage[-train_idx] auc_original <- roc(test_labels, as.numeric(test_pred))$auc print(paste("AUC:", auc_original)) #> [1] "AUC: 0.875"

title: How does harf work?
text: train {h}-ARF model using h_arf function training data, gene expression measurements provided omx_data argument, binary outcome variable addition variables including age gender passed cli_lab_data argument.
code: set.seed(seed) kich_harf <- h_arf(   omx_data = kich[train_idx , !(colnames(kich) %in% c("tumor_stage",                                                       "age", "gender"))],                    cli_lab_data = kich[train_idx, c("tumor_stage", "age", "gender")],                    chunk_size = 10,                    target = "tumor_stage",                    verbose = TRUE   ) #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 43.01% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 48.39% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 48.39% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 35.56% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 41.49% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 51.65% #> Iteration: 1, Accuracy: 39.13% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 58.51% #> Iteration: 1, Accuracy: 38.71% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 56.38% #> Iteration: 1, Accuracy: 36.56% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 55.91% #> Iteration: 1, Accuracy: 44.68% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 61.7% #> Iteration: 1, Accuracy: 32.98% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 56.99% #> Iteration: 1, Accuracy: 42.55% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 54.35% #> Iteration: 1, Accuracy: 40.86% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 55.91% #> Iteration: 1, Accuracy: 35.48% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 51.06% #> Iteration: 1, Accuracy: 44.57% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 54.95% #> Iteration: 1, Accuracy: 38.3% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 51.61% #> Iteration: 1, Accuracy: 41.94% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 56.38% #> Iteration: 1, Accuracy: 34.78% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 47.87% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 55.32% #> Iteration: 1, Accuracy: 39.36% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 57.45% #> Iteration: 1, Accuracy: 48.94% #> Iteration: 0, Accuracy: 56.52% #> Iteration: 1, Accuracy: 43.01%

title: How does harf work?
text: now use h_forge function generate synthetic training dataset.
code: # Synthetic data set.seed(seed) synth_kich <- h_forge(   harf_obj = kich_harf,   n_synth = length(train_idx),   evidence = NULL,   parallel = FALSE ) synth_kich <- as.data.frame(synth_kich)

what: Prediction model trained on the synthetic data
title: How does harf work?
text: train prediction model synthetic training data evaluate performance testing data, testing data used evaluate model trained original data. expected, prediction model trained synthetic data similar performance one trained original data, indicating synthetic datasets can used prediction tasks. illustration simulated single run, practice, users may want repeat process multiple times assess variability results. unsupervised case, users may also want tune chunk size parameter adversarial game achieve predefined level performance.
code: set.seed(seed) rf_model_synth <- ranger(tumor_stage ~ .,                           data = synth_kich,                           num.trees = 500,                           probability = FALSE) # Estimate AUC on the test set test_pred_synth <- predict(rf_model_synth, data = kich[-train_idx, ])$predictions auc_synth <- roc(test_labels, as.numeric(test_pred_synth))$auc auc_comparison <- data.frame(   Model = c("Original", "Synthetic"),   AUC = c(auc_original, auc_synth) ) print(auc_comparison) #>       Model   AUC #> 1  Original 0.875 #> 2 Synthetic 0.875

title: High-Dimensional Adversarial Random Forests
text: Adversarial random forests (ARFs) recently introduced well-performing generative method low-dimensional datasets. Based unsupervised RFs, ARFs rely recursive adversarial procedure trees progressively learn structural properties data alternating rounds data generation discrimination. unsupervised classification task achieved introducing synthetic response variable $`y \\{0,1\}`$, $`y = 0`$ denotes synthetic data $`y = 1`$ denotes original data. discrimination phase, objective distinguish original observations synthetic ones. Synthetic data created adversarial game marginal resampling original features terminal nodes. adversarial process stops prediction accuracy $`y`$ falls predefined threshold, $`0.5`$, example. ARFs demonstrated strong performance various low-dimensional settings, behavior high-dimensional contexts, omics data, remains non investigated. key assumption ARFs — feature distributions independent within terminal nodes — may violated high-dimensional settings. example, small subset features highly predictive synthetic response variable $`y`$, remaining features used reach terminal node may still correlated, allowing marginal resampling. typical resulting behaviour ARF situation algorithm terminates without converging. high-dimensional adversarial random forests (h-ARFs) package addresses convergence issue ARF identifying isolated regions feature space independence assumption likely hold terminal nodes. addition, h-ARF algorithm constructs low-dimensional meta-space captures relationships among regions. Within isolated region, separate ARFs trained better capture local data structures, followed training additional ARF meta-region capture joint structure isolated regions. synthesize observations, h-ARF algorithm first samples region meta-space condition resampling isolated region synthesized meta-observations. h-ARF algorithm offers flexible framework unconditional conditional data generation downstream clustering prediction analyses. package includes built-single-cell RNA-seq datasets TCGA-KICH dataset illustrate usage. refer package vignette detailed examples explanations.

what: High-dimensional Adversarial Random Forest (ARF) for omics and clinical data. — h_arf
title: High-dimensional Adversarial Random Forest (ARF) for omics and clinical data. — h_arf
text: algorithm extends (ARF) high-dimensional settings. partitions high-dimensional data isolated regions fits Adversarial Random Forest (ARF) models within region latent space representing region joint distribution capture within region feature dependencies.

title: High-dimensional Adversarial Random Forest (ARF) for omics and clinical data. — h_arf
code: h_arf(   omx_data,   cli_lab_data = NULL,   target = NULL,   feature_ordering = NULL,   omx_onset_data = NULL,   num_trees = 10,   min_node_size = 5,   correlation_method = "spearman",   correlation_mat = NULL,   num_btwn_pcs = 2,   num_onset_pcs = 2,   chunk_size = 10,   oob = FALSE,   family = "truncnorm",   finite_bounds = "no",   alpha = 0,   epsilon = 0,   parallel = FALSE,   export_cor_mat = FALSE,   verbose = FALSE )

title: High-dimensional Adversarial Random Forest (ARF) for omics and clinical data. — h_arf
text: omx_data data.frame containing omics data rows represent samples (e.g. patients cells) columns represent features (e.g., gene protein expressions). cli_lab_data data.frame clinical laboratory data. example, may data.frame rows represent cell types (single cell data additional clinical patient information, e.g. disease status, age, sex, BMI, etc). target Optional name target variable cli_lab_data supervised downstream analysis. NULL, training conducted unsupervised downstream analysis. feature_ordering Optional vector feature names specifying order features synthesized data. NULL, features ordered according original order omx_data, followed order clin_lab_data. omx_onset_data Optional data.frame conditional onset omics features. can used condition synthesis specific omics features (e.g. expression gene interest). provided, dimension reduction performed features resulting components included additional meta features training meta adversarial model. num_trees Number trees grow Adversarial Random Forest (ARF) model. min_node_size Minimum number samples required split internal node ARF model. correlation_method Methods compute correlation features. Options include "pearson", "spearman", "kendall". Default "spearman". correlation_mat Optional pre-computed correlation matrix features. provided, used instead computing correlations omx_data. can usefull tuning hyperparameters clustering step without recompute correlation matrix time. num_btwn_pcs Number principal components use cluster variability. Default 2. num_onset_pcs Number principal components use onset features. Default 2. chunk_size Size feature chunks process time. Default 10. oob Logical indicating whether use --bag samples density estimation. Default FALSE. Also see forde family Distribution use density estimation continuous features. See forde options. finite_bounds Impose finite bounds continuous variables? "local", infinite bounds set empirical extrema within leaves. "global", infinite bounds set global empirical extrema. "" (default), infinite bounds left unchanged. See forde details. alpha Optional pseudocount Laplace smoothing density estimation. See forde details. epsilon Optional slack parameter empirical bounds. See forde details. parallel Logical indicating whether use parallel processing. Default TRUE. export_cor_mat Logical indicating whether export correlation matrix used clustering. Default FALSE. verbose Logical indicating whether print progress messages. Default FALSE.

title: High-dimensional Adversarial Random Forest (ARF) for omics and clinical data. — h_arf
text: isoARF object containing fitted adversarial models, clustering information.

title: High-dimensional Adversarial Random Forest (ARF) for omics and clinical data. — h_arf
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ data(single_cell) harf_model <- h_arf(   omx_data = single_cell[ , - which(colnames(single_cell)  == "cell_type")],   cli_lab_data = data.frame(cell_type = single_cell$cell_type) ) # Unconditional sampling from harf_model set.seed(123) synth_single_cell <- h_forge(  harf_obj = harf_model,  n_synth = nrow(single_cell)  )  # Conditional resampling from harf_model  set.seed(142)  lung_single_cell <- h_forge(      harf_obj = harf_model,      n_synth = sum(single_cell$cell_type == "lung"),      evidence = data.frame(cell_type = "lung")     ) } # }

title: Forest generator for high-dimensional data. — h_forge
code: h_forge(   harf_obj,   n_synth,   evidence = NULL,   omx_onset_data = NULL,   evidence_row_mode = c("separate", "or"),   round = TRUE,   sample_NAs = FALSE,   nomatch = c("force", "na"),   verbose = TRUE,   stepsize = 0,   parallel = TRUE )

title: Forest generator for high-dimensional data. — h_forge
text: harf_obj pre-trained harf model. n_synth Number synthetic samples generate. evidence Optional set conditioning events. passed forde function isolated regions. See forde details. omx_onset_data Optional data.frame conditional onset omics features. evidence_row_mode Interpretation rows multi-row evidence. See forde details. round Round continuous variables respective maximum precision real data set? See forde details. sample_NAs Sample NAs respecting probability missing values original data? See forde details. nomatch leaf matches condition evidence? Options force sampling random leaf ("force") return NA ("na"). default "force". verbose leaf matches condition evidence? See forde details. stepsize many rows evidence handled step? See forde details. parallel Compute parallel? See forde details.

title: Forest generator for high-dimensional data. — h_forge
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ data(single_cell) harf_model <- h_arf(   omx_data = single_cell[ , - which(colnames(single_cell)  == "cell_type")],   cli_lab_data = data.frame(cell_type = single_cell$cell_type) ) # Unconditional sampling from harf_model set.seed(123) synth_single_cell <- h_forge(  harf_obj = harf_model,  n_synth = nrow(single_cell)  )  # Conditional resampling from harf_model  set.seed(142)  lung_single_cell <- h_forge(      harf_obj = harf_model,      n_synth = sum(single_cell$cell_type == "lung"),      evidence = data.frame(cell_type = "lung")     ) } # }
